title: Dynamic Memory 🧠
description: Ask the OS for memory on the fly.

slides.title: The Rigid Array 🧱
slides.content:
When you write `int scores[100];`, the compiler secures 100 slots of memory the exact moment the program starts.

But what if you don't know how many players will join the game? If only 2 join, you wasted 98 slots. If 200 join, the game crashes because the array is too small!

slides.title: Dynamic Memory Allocation 🏗️
slides.content:
Instead of hardcoding sizes, you can politely ask the Operating System (Windows/Mac) to generate memory *while* the program is running!

This is called **Dynamic Memory Allocation**, and we use a function called `malloc` (Memory Allocate) to do it.

slides.title: Using malloc
slides.content: `malloc` takes one argument: exactly how many TOTAL Bytes you want. It finds a block of free memory that size, and returns a Pointer to the first byte.

quizData.question: Why do we multiply `numPlayers` by `sizeof(int)` when calling `malloc`?

options.text: Because malloc doesn't know what an 'int' is. It only understands raw Bytes. We have to manually calculate how many bytes 5 ints require.
It makes the memory run faster.

quizData.explanation: `malloc(5)` would only ask for 5 Bytes. Since an integer takes 4 bytes, 5 Bytes could only hold 1 integer safely! `5 * sizeof(int)` ensures we get 20 Bytes.

quizData.question: What does `malloc` actually return back to the program after finding the requested memory?

options.text: A Pointer containing the Memory Address where the new raw block of memory begins.

quizData.explanation: Because it returns a Pointer (`int *scores`), and because array names are secretly just pointers under the hood, we can treat `scores` exactly like a normal array!
